cuePattern: Blog companion for: <exact blog title>. Prompt <prompt id>.
publicCuePolicy: The routing cue is for the pipeline only. It should be trimmed from public social exports, blog source moments, combined resource videos, and YouTube resource videos.

items.title: What's Your Retirement Number Based On?
items.routingCue: Blog companion for: What's Your Retirement Number Based On? Prompt TW-BLOG-RETNUM-01.
items.whyThisMatters: This article already has the right thesis, but it needs David on video explaining the recognition moment: a retirement number may be reasonable, but if it did not come from a plan, it is fragile.
items.whoThisIsFor: Someone 5 to 10 years from retirement who has a number in mind but is not sure what produced it.
items.storyTrigger: Someone says, "I think we need about a million dollars," but cannot explain how spending, taxes, Social Security, healthcare, account types, or risk produced that number.
items.safeStoryDoorway: Use a hypothetical person or couple. Do not imply this is one specific client. Keep the tone empathetic, not corrective.
items.firstSpokenLine: When someone tells me their retirement number is a round number, I know we probably have more work to do.
items.turn: The number might not be wrong. The problem is that they do not know whether it is right.
talkPath: A round number can come from a magazine, a friend, or a gut feeling.
The real number depends on spending, taxes, Social Security, healthcare, portfolio risk, timing, and account location.
Two people with the same balance can have very different retirement answers.
The plan should create the number, not the other way around.
items.groundedClose: The number is not the plan. The number is what falls out after the plan is honest.
doNotSay: Do not mock the person for having a round number.
Do not imply everyone needs more money.
Do not give a universal retirement target.
items.visualRefreshNotes: Turn the article into a cornerstone resource with a short-answer box, retirement-number input framework, worksheet/checklist block, related resource links, and one source moment from this recording.

items.title: What Real Tax Planning Looks Like
items.routingCue: Blog companion for: What Real Tax Planning Looks Like. Prompt TW-BLOG-REALTAX-01.
items.whyThisMatters: This is a flagship service-definition article. The companion video should make the calendar problem obvious: by March, many useful choices are already closed.
items.whoThisIsFor: Business owners, high earners, and near-retirees who only think about taxes when the return is being prepared.
items.storyTrigger: Someone asks in March what can still be done, and the answer is mostly that the planning window was months earlier.
items.safeStoryDoorway: Speak from a recurring advisory pattern, not a specific client. Be careful not to blame CPAs or tax preparers.
items.firstSpokenLine: A lot of tax planning disappointment starts with one problem: the year is already over.
items.turn: Tax preparation is necessary, but it is not the same job as tax planning.
talkPath: Tax preparation reports history.
Tax planning works while choices are still open.
Safe harbor estimates, Roth conversions, capital gains, owner pay, charitable giving, and retirement contributions all depend on timing.
The better question is what can still be changed before December 31.
items.groundedClose: The point is not to make taxes exciting. The point is to make them usable while there is still time.
doNotSay: Do not say tax preparation is bad.
Do not imply every strategy remains available all year.
Do not promise tax savings.
items.visualRefreshNotes: Add a tax-planning calendar/timeline block, decision checklist, FAQ, and related links to year-round tax planning and Keystone/Ongoing Advisory.

items.title: Should I Elect S-Corp Status?
items.routingCue: Blog companion for: Should I Elect S-Corp Status? Prompt TW-BLOG-SCORP-01.
items.whyThisMatters: The post already has good tax mechanics, but the refresh needs the more human owner-pay story: the S-Corp decision is not just a form.
items.whoThisIsFor: Profitable solo owners and small LLC owners, especially in Tennessee, who have heard an S-Corp can save taxes.
items.storyTrigger: A business owner hears "S-Corp saves taxes" and wants to flip the switch without modeling salary, state tax, payroll, and cash flow.
items.safeStoryDoorway: Use a representative owner, not a client. Keep the Tennessee nuance general and caveated.
items.firstSpokenLine: The S-Corp question usually sounds like an entity question, but it is really an owner-pay question.
items.turn: The tax savings may be real, but only after reasonable compensation, state rules, admin cost, and business durability are considered.
talkPath: Payroll tax savings come from profit above reasonable compensation.
Reasonable compensation narrows the spread and needs a defensible rationale.
Tennessee franchise and excise tax can change the net result.
Payroll, bookkeeping, tax filings, and cash-flow discipline add weight.
The business and household plan both need to support the election.
items.groundedClose: Sometimes the answer is yes. Sometimes the answer is you are buying complexity before the savings are big enough.
doNotSay: Do not give a revenue or profit threshold as a universal rule.
Do not imply all LLCs should elect S-Corp status.
Do not promise Tennessee-specific savings.
items.visualRefreshNotes: Make this a tool-backed resource with a decision framework, Tennessee caveat box, reasonable compensation checklist, S-Corp visual callout, FAQ, and source moment.

items.title: The Roth Conversion Checkbox Problem
items.routingCue: Blog companion for: The Roth Conversion Checkbox Problem. Prompt TW-BLOG-ROTHCHECK-01.
items.whyThisMatters: The article's strongest idea is emotional: people can feel responsible while still underdoing the strategy. The video should preserve that human tension.
items.whoThisIsFor: Retirees and pre-retirees with meaningful traditional IRA or 401(k) balances who are doing small Roth conversions without a lifetime plan.
items.storyTrigger: Someone proudly says they converted a little this year and believes the strategy is handled.
items.safeStoryDoorway: Use a broad pattern. Do not imply the comfortable conversion is always wrong.
items.firstSpokenLine: A lot of people are doing Roth conversions just enough to feel like the box is checked.
items.turn: The comfortable number may not be the number the lifetime tax plan would choose.
talkPath: A small conversion feels prudent because the current tax bill is visible.
The lifetime tax bill is often much larger but less emotionally present.
IRMAA, brackets, Social Security timing, RMDs, and rule changes make this a year-by-year decision.
The real question is not what feels painless this April. It is what reduces cumulative tax without creating other problems.
items.groundedClose: The right number usually is not the comfortable number. It is the number the plan can defend.
doNotSay: Do not say Roth conversions are universally good.
Do not imply larger is always better.
Do not minimize IRMAA, cash-flow, or current-year tax costs.
items.visualRefreshNotes: Preserve the POV but add a lifetime-tax framework, threshold/caveat block, FAQ, and source moment. Consider a checklist instead of a calculator unless assumptions are clear.

items.title: Tax Planning for High-Income W-2 Earners
items.routingCue: Blog companion for: Tax Planning for High-Income W-2 Earners. Prompt TW-BLOG-W2TAX-01.
items.whyThisMatters: The current article has useful strategies, but the refresh needs a clearer triage lens so W-2 earners do not just see a long list.
items.whoThisIsFor: Executives, physicians, corporate professionals, and dual-income high earners who assume the tax playbook is mostly closed to them.
items.storyTrigger: A W-2 earner compares themselves to business-owner tax content and tunes out because they think there is nothing meaningful to do.
items.safeStoryDoorway: Name the limitation honestly. Do not oversell the number of available levers.
items.firstSpokenLine: If you are a high-income W-2 earner, your tax playbook is smaller. That does not mean it is empty.
items.turn: The goal is not to collect every strategy. It is to know which few fit the person's compensation, benefits, tax bracket, and life stage.
talkPath: W-2 earners usually have fewer levers than business owners.
401(k), HSA, backdoor Roth, mega backdoor Roth, equity comp, charitable giving, deferred comp, and 529 planning can still matter.
Eligibility and timing drive the answer.
A checklist is useful only after it is filtered through the actual plan.
items.groundedClose: The point is not to collect every strategy. It is to know which few actually fit your life.
doNotSay: Do not imply every listed strategy is available.
Do not promise savings.
Do not turn this into a generic listicle hook.
items.visualRefreshNotes: Convert the article from listicle to triage guide with a fit matrix, eligibility caveats, FAQ, and related tax-planning resources.

items.title: Health Insurance Options Before Age 65
items.routingCue: Blog companion for: Health Insurance Options Before Age 65. Prompt TW-BLOG-HI65-01.
items.whyThisMatters: The article is practical, but the video should make the stakes feel real: the health insurance bridge can decide whether an early retirement date is actually workable.
items.whoThisIsFor: People considering retirement before Medicare, especially ages 60 to 64.
items.storyTrigger: A couple can afford retirement on paper, then sees the ACA or COBRA quote and feels the plan wobble.
items.safeStoryDoorway: Use a representative couple or household. Avoid giving subsidy guidance without facts.
items.firstSpokenLine: The first time someone sees the health insurance number before Medicare, retirement suddenly feels less real.
items.turn: The portfolio may be ready, but the bridge to Medicare still has to be built on purpose.
talkPath: The length of the gap changes the whole decision.
COBRA, Marketplace coverage, spouse coverage, retiree coverage, and private options each solve different problems.
Premium is not the whole cost. Doctors, prescriptions, networks, deductibles, and taxable income matter.
The healthcare decision belongs near the beginning of the retirement conversation.
items.groundedClose: I would rather find the healthcare problem before you quit than discover it after the paycheck is gone.
doNotSay: Do not imply one option is best.
Do not give subsidy advice without income facts.
Do not minimize provider-network or prescription risk.
likelyUse: Google Business Profile local retirement post
items.visualRefreshNotes: Add a coverage comparison table, pre-65 retirement checklist, source moment, FAQ, and related links to retirement income/tax planning.

items.title: What Changes as Financial Independence Gets Closer
items.routingCue: Blog companion for: What Changes as Financial Independence Gets Closer. Prompt TW-BLOG-FICLOSER-01.
items.whyThisMatters: The current article has a strong feeling but weak resource packaging. The video should name the emotional shift from accumulation to optionality.
items.whoThisIsFor: Successful households in their 50s or early 60s who have built wealth but do not yet feel free.
items.storyTrigger: Someone has enough on paper but still feels like one wrong decision could undo something important.
items.safeStoryDoorway: Keep this broad and human. Do not make financial independence sound like a luxury problem.
items.firstSpokenLine: There is a stage where your net worth gets bigger, but your decisions start feeling heavier.
items.turn: That uneasy feeling is often the signal that the old accumulation playbook is no longer enough.
talkPath: The dollars are larger, so mistakes feel more expensive.
Taxes, risk, income, estate decisions, work choices, and family priorities start interacting.
Growth still matters, but coordination starts mattering more.
Optionality is not just a number. It is knowing what choices are actually available.
items.groundedClose: That uneasy feeling is not always a warning sign. Sometimes it is the signal that the plan needs to catch up to what you have built.
doNotSay: Do not make financial independence sound like only a number.
Do not overuse lifestyle freedom language.
Do not imply the household should take less risk by default.
items.visualRefreshNotes: Add a stage-shift framework, coordination checklist, disclaimer, FAQ, related resources, and a calm CTA.

items.title: The Retirement Risk Nobody Talks About
items.routingCue: Blog companion for: The Retirement Risk Nobody Talks About. Prompt TW-BLOG-SEQRISK-01.
items.whyThisMatters: The essay has a memorable risk tension. The companion video should make the sequence-risk versus inflation-risk tradeoff easy to grasp.
items.whoThisIsFor: Near-retirees who are tempted to get overly conservative because market loss feels like the only retirement risk.
items.storyTrigger: Someone wants to move everything to cash or ultra-conservative investments because retirement is close.
items.safeStoryDoorway: Use a broad pattern. Do not recommend allocations.
items.firstSpokenLine: The hard part of retirement risk is that solving one risk badly can create another one.
items.turn: The goal is not to eliminate risk. It is to understand which risk the plan can afford to take.
talkPath: Sequence risk is real because early losses plus withdrawals can hurt.
Going too safe can create inflation risk and longevity risk.
The plan needs buffers, withdrawal rules, tax awareness, and growth calibrated to the household.
A good retirement portfolio has jobs, not just a risk score.
items.groundedClose: Retirement planning is not about hiding from risk. It is about knowing which risk you are actually taking.
doNotSay: Do not recommend a portfolio allocation.
Do not dismiss market fear.
Do not imply risk can be eliminated.
items.visualRefreshNotes: Keep the essay feel but add a two-risk comparison table, FAQ, related links, and one source moment.

items.title: The Tax Strategy Gap
items.routingCue: Blog companion for: The Tax Strategy Gap. Prompt TW-BLOG-TAXGAP-01.
items.whyThisMatters: The post has a strong emotional hook, but the refresh needs the moment where a successful person cannot name the strategy behind a large tax bill.
items.whoThisIsFor: High earners, owners, and families with meaningful tax bills but no visible tax strategy.
items.storyTrigger: A prospect says the tax number out loud and shrugs because they do not know what else could have been done.
items.safeStoryDoorway: Do not shame the person and do not bash CPAs. Frame this as a common planning gap.
items.firstSpokenLine: When I ask someone what tax strategy they are using, the silence tells me a lot.
items.turn: A large tax bill is not automatically a mistake, but it should not be a mystery.
talkPath: People confuse accounts, deductions, or filing accuracy with strategy.
Tax strategy has three jobs: avoidance, timing, and cash-flow management.
Different strategies apply at different income levels and life stages.
Someone has to own the question of what applies, when it applies, and what needs to be implemented.
items.groundedClose: If the tax bill is big enough to bother you, it is big enough to deserve a plan.
doNotSay: Do not shame the taxpayer.
Do not imply every large tax bill means bad advice.
Do not promise hundreds of thousands in savings as a typical result.
items.visualRefreshNotes: Remove repetitive copy, add a three-jobs framework, answer box, FAQ, related links, and a measured CTA.
